vision:
I make images. Each one starts with the day's news and ends in South LA. The practice is daily: I read what is happening in the world and ask what it would look like if it arrived here, on asphalt I know, in the light of a specific afternoon. It's not illustration. The news story is a starting condition, not a subject. I use AI image generation, and I treat it the way artists have always treated new tools: the question is not whether it is legitimate, it is what it makes possible that wasn't possible before. 


What it makes possible is cadence. One image a day, each answering the world at the speed the world actually moves. The danger in that speed is that it becomes the whole point, that the images pile up and cancel each other out. I work against that. I want each one to slow you down, to hold its complexity instead of flattening it into a headline you've already scrolled past. 


The formal work is in the translation: what in the story survives the move, what gets clarified by landing here rather than somewhere abstract. I am interested in images that don't give everything up immediately. Beauty is part of how that works, but not beauty that resolves. The picture should hold a tension it doesn't explain. 


The world sends the story. I decide where it lands, and how long it makes you stand there.

vince.body:
Light came in low this morning over the boulevard. I kept thinking about the couple on the motorcycle, the small altar someone had built on the curb.

I worked the scene until the asphalt felt like skin. Then I stopped.
A vigil at Alvarado and 7th. The candles flickered against the bus shelter. I did not want to make a picture of grief, only of the light around it.

ted.body:
Two gallerists wrote back today. One wants to see the new sequence in person.

I told Vince. He shrugged, which is his version of yes.

correspondence.body:
Dear Vince,

The collector in Mexico City asked again about the motorcycle piece. She wants to know if there will be a second in the series. I told her you don't work that way, but I'm telling you now in case you do.

Eat something today.

Ever yours,
Ted

Dear Ted,

There will not be a second. There may be a different one, later, that rhymes with it. Tell her that if you must tell her anything.

I ate. Eggs, twice.

Your brother,
Vince
